cards.back: An identifier's linkage determines whether multiple declarations of the same identifier refer to the same object or not.
An identifier with internal linkage can be seen and used within a single translation unit, but is not accessible from other translation units.

cards.text: To make a global variable internal, use the {{c1::static}} keyword before the variable definition.

cards.front: What is the default linkage for const global variables?
cards.back: Internal linkage. Const global variables have internal linkage by default (unless explicitly declared extern).

cards.front: What is the default linkage for constexpr variables?
cards.back: Internal linkage. Constexpr variables are implicitly const and therefore have internal linkage by default.

cards.text: To make a function internal to a file, use the {{c1::static}} keyword before the function definition: {{c2::static void helper() { }}}

cards.question: Which of these has internal linkage?
cards.code:
int g_a;                    // A
static int g_b;             // B
const int g_c{ 1 };         // C
constexpr int g_d{ 2 };     // D
cards.answer: B, C, and D have internal linkage
cards.explanation: B has internal linkage due to 'static'. C has internal linkage because const globals default to internal. D has internal linkage because constexpr implies const. Only A has external linkage.

cards.front: Can you define an identifier with internal linkage in multiple files?
cards.back: Yes. Because each definition is internal to its own translation unit, multiple files can have identically-named identifiers with internal linkage without violating the one-definition rule.

cards.front: Why use static (internal linkage) for helper functions?
cards.back: To prevent naming collisions across files and to indicate that a function is only used within its translation unit (not part of the public interface).
A source file plus all of its included headers. Each .cpp file becomes a separate translation unit.

cards.text: Use {{c1::internal linkage}} (via static or const) when you want a global identifier to be used only within a single file. This prevents {{c2::naming collisions}} across files.

cards.explanation:
Use internal linkage for:
  • Helper functions used only in one file
  • Global constants that don't need to be shared
  • Implementation details that shouldn't be accessible from other files
  • Preventing naming conflicts in larger projects

cards.text: Const global variables have {{c1::internal linkage}} by default, while non-const globals have {{c2::external linkage}}.
Constexpr variables are implicitly {{c1::const}} and therefore have {{c2::internal linkage}} by default.
To make a function internal to a file, use {{c1::static}} before the function: static void {{c2::helper}}() { }
A {{c1::translation unit}} is a source file plus all of its {{c2::included headers}}. Each .cpp file becomes a separate translation unit.
